sentences.text: The Jewish police.
I remember the name of the policeman.
He was an officer in the hierarchy of the Jewish police in Plaszow.
Marcel ?] Goldberg is his name.
And he beat me up terribly.
He used to carry one of those, I don't know what you call these, whips.
And he was a big man, you know, because he had a whip.
So he used it.
No, because he-- at that point, he brought me in to the office of the Jewish police, because they had the responsibility to deliver all the people to the transport.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they didn't-- they didn't even mix into this, the Germans.
It was all his doing, was his responsibility to bring me into the-- onto the train, to the transport.
Yeah, a very short time.
Only three days.
It was every day the same thing.
We-- the worst part of it was the uncertainty.
What is going to happen?
What is going to happen tomorrow or this afternoon?
The-- I knew that on the rations that we are getting there, one piece of bread and a little bit of soup in the evening, you cannot live for a long time.
But I think my wish came-- came true when, after only three days, they shipped me out from there.
And they took-- and they transported a large group of people into this place called Wüstegiersdorf.
This was a new labor camp.
I couldn't say, because I didn't drive that.
[LAUGHS]
No, it was a short distance.
It was all in Silesia.
Yes, it wasn't far.
Anyway, they brought us there to a completely new labor camp.
They were building new barracks for-- to hold the people, the-- the-- the laborers-- the laborers.
And they needed also facilities for an organization, a German organization, OT, which stands for Organisation Todt.
People who are familiar with the-- with the German establishment knew that-- who they were.
They were a branch of the military, but not directly involved, like the SS or the Wehrmacht.
They had other functions.
They needed offices.
They also had large stables of horses in that place.

So they needed people who would attend the horses, to build the barracks, to-- since I was already established as an electrician, so-- and a mechanic, so they put me to work as an electrician to make new installations of-- of electric wiring into-- in the new barracks.
And this, as I said, was a completely new camp.
There were new-- new bunks.
And they were pretty well equipped with sacks-- with straw sacks.
And-- and the conditions looked completely different.
And we also had showers.
We could shower every day.
[INAUDIBLE]
Well there were-- I don't remember the size of the barrack.
I only know, in the other camps where we were cramped barracks, like in Gross-Rosen, that then we were over-- much overcrowded.
But here, there was a regular barracks of maybe 50 or 60.
I-- I couldn't really tell.
But they were comfortable.
Everybody has his own bunk, and he had a blanket, as I recall also.
And the most important thing was the showers.
We could take a shower every day.
It was unreal.
And then the working conditions.
We're going out with a kommando of 20.
My kommando was 20 people.
All were specialists, so to speak.
There was a carpenter, there was an electrician, there were other specialists.
And we were going out under the supervision of one person only, one soldier, a Wehrmacht.
He was not an SS.
He was a regular soldier of the German army.
And he was, they called it, an [GERMAN].
He was our supervisor.
He was leading us--
No.
I don't think I know his name.
He was leading us to the work place, and bring us back in the evening.
All day long, we didn't even see him.
And everybody was doing his work.
And over there, we had a lot of good contacts with German civilians.
And we developed a relationship with some.
They used to bring us tomatoes and apples, and extra bread.
And it was-- it was like a paradise on Earth compared to everything else.

And I think this period of six months in this Wüstegiersdorf sustained me so that I could-- I was able to survive the rest, the hardships, of the-- of the last three months of the war, which was absolutely horrible, which I'll describe later.
Yes.
Well, of course they knew.
Of course they knew.
But we wouldn't want to talk about politics with them.
We wouldn't want to antagonize them.
We were grateful for the handout that we were getting from them.
And we didn't go under-- but in-- in-- at one occasion, I remember I had access to an office.
And I-- there was a radio, and I could listen to BBC.
I just caught a short time of a transmission from BBC.
And now-- in this-- and we got to know the conditions in-- of the war.
This was already in 1944.
The things didn't go well for the Germans already then, but we really didn't have any way of knowing that.
And this was a very good source of information.

Another thing which I had there, which was a racket, I would call it, was going on for me, is, because I was an electrician, I had access to every place-- cellars-- where there were these circuit breakers.
Usually they don't have them, yeah.
So I used to artificially break the circuits so to be able to go down to the circuit breaker, because I found that one of these cellars had a large supply of cognac.
Cognac from-- from France.
They were packed in very large, narrow crates-- wooden crates.
And I was-- I opened one of these and I said, it's cognac.
I was able to get a bottle of cognac every day-- almost every day-- from that, and bring it to the camp.
You can imagine, I have a bottle of cognac, and I sold it for-- for other goodies from the kitchen.
And it-- it-- I cannot really describe it, the-- the windfall.
And not only myself, because others, also.
When I got used to this routine, I got more than one bottle.
And others were carrying them to the camp.
So we had, sometimes, three or four bottles of cognac, bringing into the camp.
People who work in the kitchen.
No.
Other prisoners.
Who worked in the kitchen had access to stuff in the kitchen.
So we bartered cognac for other stuff.
For soup, for bread, for cheese-- not cheese, but they had these-- I don't remember what it was.
Anyway, there was-- there were things in the kitchen which they-- they bartered with us.
No.
Just the top of my-- I saw the opportunity.
They needed an electrician.
I am the one.
I didn't know what at first.
Let me-- this is interesting.
At first, I was working under the supervision of a young German who was an electrician.
And I was working with him maybe for three months.
And I learned a lot from him.
I really became an electrician under his supervision.
Then he was called into the army, and I was the only one in charge of everything.
So he gave me the keys, because there was a, you know, a storage room where they had the circuit breakers, wiring, bulb-- light bulbs, and other things which are necessary to-- for-- to maintain it.
I was all of a sudden in charge of everything.
And when the [GERMAN], the soldier who was in charge of our-- of our transport, he wanted a light bulb, he had to come to me and give me bread for it.
And I sold him-- the German-- light bulbs.
This is the situation.
Of course, you cannot compare.
This was not a concentration camp.
It was a work camp, and the conditions were very lenient.
And there was not much supervision from the SS.
It was just the Organisation Todt who had things to say.
It was for their benefit that we worked for-- for them.
Only.
Only Jewish men.
No other nationalities there.
No.
Not from there.
Why-- why escape from that kind of--
Yeah.
Much better.
No.
No.
But there are people here who survived that camp who I am in touch with.
We sometimes reminisce about the times there in Wüstegiersdorf.
Made friends there, yeah.
And in fact, after-- this lasted for nine months-- and it was exactly February 16, 1945.
We were evacuated from Wüstegiersdorf and march westward into Germany.
The destination then was Flossenburg.
We didn't know that, of course, but now I know that this was-- the period after Wüstegiersdorf was Flossenburg.
But this was going on for weeks.
We were marching and marching.
Of course, Flossenburg is in Bavaria, and this is in Silesia, which is eastern.
Oh, hundreds and hundreds of miles.
Yeah.
It began in February 16.
Now, at the beginning, everybody was-- was very, very good shape.
First of all, health-wise we were all right.
We were not so undernourished.
And we had-- we were equipped with some-- with bread on-- for the march, and other stuff that we had.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So most of us survived that march, because of the physical condition that we were in.
But I had one-- one good friend, which I developed a very close relationship, you know, in that Wüstegiersdorf.
We were walking, and he dropped dead on side of me.
He couldn't.
There was something wrong with him.
Maybe he was sick.
We couldn't establish this.
Anyway, he dropped-- on our march, he dropped dead.
I took it very hard, because we-- really we became very, very close friends.
So these few weeks on the march, we were sustained by the-- by the compar-- comparatively good health that we were in, and also the provisions that we had with us.
Because on the way, we were sleeping outside on the grass.
There were all kinds of weather.
There were snow falls, and there were drizzles, and there were-- because it was in February.
We started out in February, and we came to Flossenburg the beginning of April.
So you marched from February till--
Yeah.
Right.
Germans.
How many?
I don't know how many.
At least 200.
Yeah.
Yes, of course.
They had also to sleep.
They-- they didn't have it any better, because they were outside.
They couldn't have-- they couldn't find any [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] place for their-- you know, to sleep.
They slept outside the same way as we did.
No they didn't-- they didn't shoot.
No, they didn't shoot him, because these were not SS.
They were the Wehrmacht, the soldiers, the military people.
And by then, these-- they were not young men anymore, because they were older men who were drafted in, you know, to do the other works that they couldn't send up to the front.
And they were a completely different type of people.
They looked away on any-- on many things.
So the hardship for the march was just the march itself and the weather.
But there was no danger of somebody being shot or [INAUDIBLE] things like this.
I don't remember.
I think we-- we-- we were very well equipped, very well equipped.
I mean, we had-- we were using-- wearing these striped suits.
But there were different suits than we got in-- yeah.
Better suits.
Yeah, they were-- really, you could get-- they were lined.
They were different than the ones were distributed to us in Gross-Rosen.
Because when we came to this Wüstegiersdorf, we-- we got new-- new-- everything new.
Because they had better quality stuff for the German prisoners, and this is what we must have gotten there.
February 16 to the beginning of April.
Yes.
About six weeks.
Then we came to Flossenburg.
Which is in Bavaria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We-- they took us in to a camp within a camp.
They called it the [GERMAN], Transit Camp.
Flossenburg was a very large camp.
But this here were only three large barracks.
They were surrounded with barbed wire, and it was called the Transit Camp.
And this is where they brought us in.
Over there, they start everything from the beginning.
Again, you get undressed.
They discarded everything.
They took everything away from you.
And then again they shaved our heads and they put us through the showers.
And then, we were put in into barracks, which were 400 beds for 1,200 people.
Our group was a small group, but there were others already there.
So there were four people to a barrack-- to a-- to a bunk.
So everybody had only just the corner of a bunk.
This is how you spend your night.
So at 4:00 in the morning, the kapo-- you're familiar with that word, kapo?
The-- the overseer and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] turned on the light.
Everybody up, down from the bunks.
And everybody raus.
Raus means get out of the barracks.
There are 1,200 people in the barracks.
The openings, the gates out-- to go out was about three yards wide.
And here 1,200 people push against a small opening.
The kapos-- there were 10 kapos in every barrack-- were lined up on each side.
And they had [INAUDIBLE] and-- and whips.
And they were hitting people, while the people were pushing to get out to make it faster, faster, faster, you know?
And they were hitting people from all sides.
They didn't look anybody-- to hit somebody in particular.
But you couldn't avoid getting hit.
If you were-- you want to push out, and you had to push out.
Once we were out, we had to line up in an orderly fashion.
And they needed 20 volunteers to go to the kitchen to bring coffee.
That's what they said.
Well, there were no volunteers so they picked 20 people.
They marched into the kitchen, which was outside of that camp.
It was in the larger camp, because this one didn't have a kitchen.
And when they-- when they came back, every two people carried a large canister of black water-- like coffee.
There were-- these maybe 50 liters canister, so tall.
They needed two people to carry.
They lined up 10 canisters, one next to the other.
And now everybody was supposed to come close to get a-- a ladle full of coffee.
But there was always a skirmish.
Everybody-- you know, people want to get ahead.
People want to get it sooner.
And there was [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, you're going to be punished.
He took all the 10 canisters and turned it upside.
They poured the coffee out.
This was the end of the coffee.
Nobody got any coffee.
This happened not once, not twice.
Every day.
Every day, they brought 10 canisters of coffee, and they poured it out to the ground.
Nobody got coffee.
This was the cruelty of-- of-- of Flossenburg.
I mean, probably every camp had its own.
But this was-- OK.
So no coffee.
We stayed out in the cold outside, and we try to warm, standing one next to the other.
And then at about 12:00, they chased us in to the barracks.
They're bringing soup.
Same story with the-- like with the coffee, they needed volunteers, and they brought the soup.
And they-- they pushed the-- all in the-- in the barracks to one side.
They lined up the canisters with the soup in the center.
And people would come over from one side, get their soup, and go to the other side.
So they had to control that one wouldn't get soup twice.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The soup was handed out.
And you came with your-- you had some kind of a container for the soup.
You came-- if you came too close to-- to the-- to the canister-- you know, too close, just too close-- he hit you over the head.
What are you-- you know.
If you were too far away, he dropped the soup on the floor.
This was going on all the time.
This was the kapos.
These-- they were handing out the soup.
So if you were a little off, not so close enough, so instead of dropping the soup into your container, they dropped it on the floor.
It happened to me.

It happened to a lot of people, because they-- they were finding ways to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] reduce you, you know, to-- to just-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes I'm very fluent and it comes to this and I'm missing words.
Anyway, this procedure was over.
In the afternoon, they chased us out of the barracks again.
If it was nice weather outside, they left us stay inside.
If it was nasty, they chased us out.
And about 6:00, into the barracks again, and there was distribution of bread in the evening.
In the morning was supposed to be the coffee, and then the soup, and then the bread.
So everybody got a slice of bread, like the Wonder Bread you have-- you see here in these packages.
Something of that kind.
This-- one slice of bread.
Black bread, something.
We don't know what it was made of.
And this was it.
During the night, if you wanted-- you had to get up, to get out to the latrine, the latrine was full of dead people-- stacked up.
Naked-- naked corpses, stacked up like wood, one on top of the other, because people were dying during the night.
And they were pulling them out, undress them, put them-- stacked them up, because, you know, after a couple of hours, the body gets stiff.
And they were stacking up just like wood, this way and this way.
So when you got in during the night, [INAUDIBLE] you'll-- you will find all these corpses.
Every night.
Yeah.
Every-- every day.
In the morning, we had to carry these corpses out to the field.
And they burned them, because they didn't--
I had to do that.
They didn't have crematoriums there.
They burned bodies in the open field.
So after the night, there was always a lot of people, a lot of dead people.
We carried them out, and they put them to fire.
They burned them.
There was always smoke going and smoldering, always.
Day and night.
I-- no.
I didn't-- I didn't-- I don't recall knowing anybody.
Somehow you become so isolated inward that you-- it's very seldom.
There are people who made and carried over some friendships, some-- from that time.
I only remember that fellow I told you that was in Wüstegiersdorf.
But I don't remember him from later.
I remember him from Wüstegiersdorf.
I don't remember him from later on.
You had-- you had hardly strength to talk.
People were dying next to you.
You know, and the next day, you didn't see the people that you saw yesterday.
You don't know who is-- who was gone, who was--
No, no.
No.
No.
You had to get out of the barracks, stay outside, then come in for the soup, stay outside, and this is how it was.
No.
No.
No Appelplatz.
There was, in the barracks, the one in charge, you know, woke us up in the morning, raus.
And then this is the routine that went on every day.
Then they pick people to carry the corpses out, you know, from the latrine, to carry them out into the field.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, I was three months.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] No, no, no.
Three weeks, sorry, because I came the beginning of April.
And then, towards the end of April, must be in the 22nd, 23rd, 24th, they were marching us out.
They were marching us to Allach.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, this is where I was liberated.
In Flossenburg until a few days before liberation.
No.
[INAUDIBLE]
Right.
Five days probably.
It was already the last leg.
We-- we were so exhausted.
It was-- we-- we didn't really care what is happening.
The simple-- everything is-- everything is the same.
It's just-- we didn't believe we were going to be liberated.
We didn't know that the war is going to an end.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And how can you describe feelings of this kind?
So you marched for about five days to--
Yeah.
To Allach.
Allach is not far from Munich, and not far from Dachau.
It was a camp, a subsidiary of Dachau.
It was like another camp with barracks.
And we came here.
And there, the last-- last, I think, two nights we slept in these barracks.
No idea.
No idea.
No, it wasn't crowded anymore.
It wasn't crowded.
Conditions were just the same, only it was less organized.
It-- you were left alone to yourself.
For the two days, you didn't have the supervision as you had in Flossenburg.
And this gave us a-- an indication that something is changing.
There must be an end or something.
So we didn't know what, but we didn't have-- there was no SS in that.
There were only the old soldiers, elderly people.
And you didn't-- you didn't see them around much.
And you were on your own really all day long.
You could be outside.
You could be inside.
Nobody was chasing you.
Nobody's telling you what to do.
But this was only two days, probably.
Maybe three.
There, I didn't see any.
No.
No.
Yes.
It was not organized.
But we-- we-- I think it was-- we had access to the kitchen.
It was so disorganized that we had access to the kitchen, and we got-- we were able to get food.
